meta.dataset: FloraLexicon North America — USA & Canada Ethnobotany
meta.coverage: United States and Canada — Indigenous and early settler medicinal plants
sources: Moerman, D.E. — Native American Ethnobotany Database (NAEB), University of Michigan-Dearborn
United Plant Savers (UpS) Species At-Risk List 2022
USDA PLANTS Database — plant guides and factsheets
NPS Petroglyph National Monument — Medicinal Plants of the SW
U.S. Forest Service — Native Plant Materials & Wildflowers
NLM Native Voices Exhibition — Healing Plants
Wikipedia — Native American Ethnobotany (compiled ethnobotanical references)
Provided compiled list of 60 Native North American medicinal herbs with HTML table data
meta.notes: harvest_status reflects United Plant Savers (UpS) At-Risk designations and general wildcrafting ethics. native_status: 'native' = indigenous to N. America; 'naturalized' = introduced from Europe/Asia but integrated into Indigenous and settler traditions. Regions: NE=Northeast, SE=Southeast, GP=Great Plains, SW=Southwest, PNW=Pacific Northwest, RC=Rocky/Columbia Plateau, AK=Alaska/Boreal Canada, CA=California, NA=widespread

plants.habitat: Prairies, open woods, meadows — central and eastern North America
plants.traditional_uses: Immune stimulant; colds, flu, wounds, infections, snake bite (Plains tribes, Cherokee)
plants.harvest_notes: Overharvested commercially; buy cultivated only (UpS At-Risk List)
plants.safety: Generally safe; rare allergic reactions in those with Asteraceae sensitivity

plants.habitat: Rich, shaded deciduous forests — Appalachians, Midwest, SE Canada
plants.traditional_uses: Adaptogen; fatigue, immune support, vitality, longevity tonic (Cherokee, Iroquois, Ojibwe)
plants.harvest_notes: Federally regulated for export (CITES Appendix II); severely threatened by overharvesting and habitat loss
plants.safety: Avoid with blood thinners, stimulants; not for children or pregnancy

plants.habitat: Rich, moist woodland floors — eastern USA, Ontario
plants.traditional_uses: Antimicrobial; digestive complaints, eye/skin infections, gonorrhea, mouth sores (Cherokee, Iroquois)
plants.harvest_notes: Threatened in Canada (COSEWIC); UpS Watch List; do not wild-harvest — use cultivated
plants.safety: Not for pregnancy; avoid long-term use; interacts with blood thinners and blood pressure medications

plants.habitat: Hardwood forests — eastern North America
plants.traditional_uses: Women's health; menstrual cramps, menopausal symptoms, childbirth, rheumatism, sore throats, kidney problems (Iroquois, Cherokee)
plants.harvest_notes: UpS At-Risk List; heavily commercialized; buy cultivated only
plants.safety: Not for pregnancy; potential hepatotoxicity with long-term high doses; consult physician if liver conditions

plants.habitat: Moist forest floors — eastern North America
plants.traditional_uses: Topical antiseptic, emetic, respiratory aid, ceremonial dye, tooth/gum conditions (Iroquois, Algonquin, Cherokee)
plants.harvest_notes: UpS At-Risk List; slow reproducer; do not wild-harvest casually
plants.safety: TOXIC internally in large doses — sanguinarine alkaloid; external/low-dose use only; not for pregnancy

plants.habitat: Rich bottomlands and woodlands — eastern and central USA and Canada
plants.traditional_uses: Sore throat, cough, digestive irritation, diarrhea, wounds, birthing aid (Cherokee, Iroquois, Chippewa)
plants.harvest_notes: UpS At-Risk List; harvesting bark kills the tree; Dutch elm disease has further reduced populations — use only sustainably sourced
plants.safety: Generally safe; may slow absorption of medications if taken together

plants.habitat: Prairies, uplands, thickets, open woods — Canada to southern USA
plants.traditional_uses: Antiseptic, fever reducer; colds, headaches, digestive issues, coughs, skin infections (Ojibwe, Blackfoot, Crow)
plants.harvest_notes: Not listed at-risk; grows readily in cultivation; harvest sustainably from abundant stands
plants.safety: Generally safe; avoid high doses in pregnancy (emmenagogue properties)

plants.habitat: Fields, roadsides, disturbed ground — widespread across North America
plants.traditional_uses: Wound healing, styptic, fever, anti-inflammatory, digestive (used by 60+ Indigenous nations)
plants.harvest_notes: Abundant and widespread; harvest responsibly from unsprayed areas
plants.safety: Avoid in pregnancy; rare allergic reaction in Asteraceae-sensitive individuals

plants.habitat: Moist thickets, streamsides, low areas — eastern and central North America
plants.traditional_uses: Immune support, colds, flu, fevers, infections, skin conditions (Potawatomi, Cherokee, Iroquois)
plants.harvest_notes: Not at-risk; grows abundantly in moist habitats; cook berries before consuming
plants.safety: Raw berries/bark/leaves cause nausea — cook before use; do not confuse with toxic red elderberry (S. racemosa)

plants.habitat: Wet meadows, streambanks — eastern and central North America
plants.traditional_uses: Flu, fever, bone pain, respiratory infections (Delaware, Cherokee, Algonquin)
plants.harvest_notes: Not at-risk; harvest from abundant stands sustainably
plants.safety: Avoid in pregnancy and with liver conditions; some pyrrolizidine alkaloids — moderate use only

plants.habitat: Rich woodland soils — eastern North America
plants.traditional_uses: Uterine tonic, labor aid, rheumatism, menstrual cramps, throat complaints (Cherokee, Ojibwe, Iroquois)
plants.harvest_notes: UpS At-Risk List; avoid wild harvest
plants.safety: AVOID in pregnancy (labor stimulant); toxic in large doses — cardiovascular and uterine stimulant

plants.habitat: Moist woods, streambanks — eastern North America
plants.traditional_uses: Nervine; anxiety, stress, insomnia, nervous tension, rabies fear (Cherokee, Iroquois)
plants.harvest_notes: Not currently at-risk but populations declining; prefer cultivated sources
plants.safety: Generally safe at recommended doses; potential hepatotoxicity from adulterated commercial products; avoid in pregnancy

plants.habitat: Fields, roadsides, disturbed areas — southeastern USA north to Ohio
plants.traditional_uses: Sedative; anxiety, insomnia, hypertension, convulsions (Cherokee, Houma)
plants.harvest_notes: Not at-risk; grows readily; edible fruit
plants.safety: Mild sedative — avoid with sedative medications; not for pregnancy

plants.habitat: Mountain meadows, 6000-10000 ft — Rocky Mountains, SW USA
plants.traditional_uses: Respiratory infections, coughs, antiviral, fever, bronchitis (Navajo, Apache, Pueblo)
plants.harvest_notes: High-altitude populations slow to regrow; harvest only mature plants; leave most behind
plants.safety: Avoid in pregnancy (uterine stimulant); do not confuse with toxic poison hemlock (Conium maculatum)

plants.habitat: Conifer forests — Pacific Northwest and Rockies
plants.traditional_uses: Antimicrobial; digestive issues, skin infections, liver tonic, fever (Salish, Flathead, Nlaka'pamux)
plants.harvest_notes: Abundant in Pacific Northwest forests; harvest sustainably; contains berberine
plants.safety: Avoid in pregnancy; do not use in infants or with jaundice

plants.habitat: Moist, dense forests — Pacific Northwest, Alaska, western Canada
plants.traditional_uses: Adaptogen; blood sugar regulation, respiratory illness, arthritis, infections, spiritual protection (Haida, Tlingit, Tsimshian)
plants.harvest_notes: Culturally sensitive; very spiny — harvest carefully; slow-growing; prefer cultivated
plants.safety: TOXIN WARNING: spines cause infection; internal use must be guided by knowledgeable healer; avoid in pregnancy

plants.habitat: Shady woodlands — eastern Canada, eastern and central USA
plants.traditional_uses: Digestive aid, headache, fever, earache, heart conditions, coughs, colds (Iroquois, Ojibwe, Algonquin)
plants.harvest_notes: Slow-growing woodland species; prefer cultivated; contains aristolochic acid analogs
plants.safety: Contains aristolochic acid analogs — nephrotoxic with long-term use; NOT for extended internal use; avoid in pregnancy and kidney conditions

plants.habitat: Moist disturbed sites, riparian areas — widespread North America
plants.traditional_uses: Anti-inflammatory, arthritis, allergies, kidney tonic, iron-rich food, hair care (Ojibwe, many nations)
plants.harvest_notes: Abundant and widespread; harvest young leaves with gloves; cooking/drying neutralizes sting
plants.safety: Use gloves when harvesting; root may interact with blood pressure and prostate medications

plants.habitat: Open woods, fields, meadows — eastern North America
plants.traditional_uses: Respiratory antispasmodic, asthma, bronchitis, emetic (Cherokee, Iroquois, Penobscot)
plants.harvest_notes: Use with caution; declining in some areas; medicinal use only with guidance
plants.safety: TOXIC in large doses — lobeline causes nausea, vomiting, respiratory depression; narrow therapeutic window; avoid in pregnancy, heart conditions

plants.habitat: Rich, moist woodlands — eastern North America
plants.traditional_uses: Uterine tonic, childbirth aid, hemorrhage, skin infections, menstrual disorders (Cherokee, Ojibwe, Iroquois)
plants.harvest_notes: UpS At-Risk List; extremely slow growing — harvesting root kills plant; never wild-harvest
plants.safety: Avoid in pregnancy except under skilled guidance near term; saponin-rich — moderate toxicity

plants.habitat: Forest floors — eastern North America
plants.traditional_uses: Labor preparation, UTIs, menstrual regulation, childbirth (Cherokee, Iroquois, Ojibwe)
plants.harvest_notes: Low-growing woodland plant; slow to recover; use sustainably or cultivated
plants.safety: Avoid in early pregnancy; traditionally used only near term

plants.habitat: Marshes, streamsides, wet soils — widespread North America
plants.traditional_uses: Digestive stimulant, fever, cold, toothache, ceremonial (Cree, Ojibwe, Penobscot — extremely widespread use)
plants.harvest_notes: Tetraploid North American variety; some alkaloid concerns; use moderately
plants.safety: Contain asarone analogs — avoid long-term heavy use internally; FDA restricted food additive use

plants.habitat: Woodlands — eastern and central North America, Canada
plants.traditional_uses: Blood purifier, tonic, skin eruptions, respiratory, rheumatism (Ojibwe, Cree, Algonquin)
plants.harvest_notes: Widespread in boreal and eastern forests; not at-risk
plants.safety: Generally safe; root contains saponins — moderate use

plants.habitat: Rich shaded woods — eastern and central North America
plants.traditional_uses: Expectorant, back pain, rheumatism, blood purifier, skin conditions, birthing (Iroquois, Cherokee, Ojibwe)
plants.harvest_notes: Populations declining in some areas; wild-harvest cautiously or use cultivated
plants.safety: Generally safe; large doses emetic

plants.habitat: Dry, sandy, acidic soils — boreal/temperate northern habitats
plants.traditional_uses: Urinary tract and bladder infections, smoking mix (kinnikinnick), diuretic (Blackfoot, Ojibwe, Cree, many nations)
plants.harvest_notes: Not at-risk but slow-growing groundcover; harvest sustainably; do not strip whole plants
plants.safety: Avoid with kidney disease; limit use to 2 weeks; contains arbutin/hydroquinone — avoid long-term

plants.habitat: Rich forest floors — eastern and central USA
plants.traditional_uses: Snakebite, fever, digestive stimulant, childbirth (Cherokee, various SE nations)
plants.harvest_notes: UpS Watch List; contains aristolochic acid — use with extreme caution; rare in wild
plants.safety: HIGH CAUTION: aristolochic acid is nephrotoxic and carcinogenic; avoid internal use; historical use only

plants.habitat: Dry meadows, prairies, open areas — eastern and central North America
plants.traditional_uses: Pleurisy, bronchitis, pneumonia, diarrhea, dysentery, rheumatism (Cherokee, Navajo, Choctaw)
plants.harvest_notes: Milkweed populations are declining; critical monarch butterfly habitat; harvest root only from abundant stands
plants.safety: Do not confuse with toxic Asclepias species; this species considered relatively safe; avoid in pregnancy

plants.habitat: Dry open woods, pine barrens — eastern USA
plants.traditional_uses: Immune stimulant, lymphatic tonic, fevers, acute infections (Cherokee, Delaware, Mohegan)
plants.harvest_notes: Not at-risk but slow-growing; prefer cultivated; wild populations declining
plants.safety: Toxic in large doses; nausea and vomiting; short-term use only; avoid in pregnancy and autoimmune conditions

plants.habitat: Dry open woods, rocky slopes — eastern and central North America
plants.traditional_uses: Lymphatic tonic, sore throat, respiratory, blood cleanser, sedative (Iroquois, Cherokee, Ojibwe)
plants.harvest_notes: Widespread shrub; leaves used as tea substitute during American Revolution; not at-risk
plants.safety: Generally safe; root contains ceanothine — may affect blood clotting; avoid with anticoagulants

plants.habitat: Moist woodlands and meadows — eastern North America
plants.traditional_uses: Uterine tonic, menstrual irregularities, threatened miscarriage, digestive (Cherokee, Iroquois)
plants.harvest_notes: UpS At-Risk List; slow-growing, dioecious — populations vulnerable; cultivate only
plants.safety: Saponin-rich; avoid in pregnancy except near term under skilled supervision

plants.habitat: Dry coniferous forests — North America and Eurasia
plants.traditional_uses: Urinary conditions, kidney tonic, back pain, rheumatism, fevers (Cree, Ojibwe, Blackfoot)
plants.harvest_notes: Slow-growing evergreen groundcover; UpS Watch List; prefer cultivated
plants.safety: Contains hydroquinone derivatives — avoid with kidney disease; limit use; not for pregnancy

plants.habitat: Dry slopes, chaparral, canyons — California and southern Oregon
plants.traditional_uses: Respiratory infections, asthma, coughs, tuberculosis, fevers, rheumatism (Costanoan, Yokuts, Ohlone)
plants.harvest_notes: Abundant in California chaparral; not at-risk
plants.safety: Generally safe; potential for contact dermatitis from resinous leaves

plants.habitat: Coastal and western North American forests — Pacific Northwest
plants.traditional_uses: Laxative (one of the most important Native NW laxative plants), liver tonic (Nlaka'pamux, Haida)
plants.harvest_notes: UpS At-Risk List in some regions; heavily commercialized; check local status
plants.safety: CRITICAL: bark must be aged 1+ year or treated with heat — fresh bark causes severe vomiting; avoid in pregnancy, bowel obstruction, inflammatory bowel; do not use long-term

plants.habitat: Dry to moist coniferous and mixed forests — eastern North America
plants.traditional_uses: Pain relief (contains methyl salicylate), fever, respiratory, arthritis, headaches (Ojibwe, Iroquois, Cree)
plants.harvest_notes: Widespread and abundant; not at-risk
plants.safety: High methyl salicylate content in essential oil — TOXIC if oil consumed directly; avoid with aspirin sensitivity or anticoagulants; safe in tea form

plants.habitat: Moist woodland edges — eastern North America
plants.traditional_uses: Astringent, skin conditions, bruises, inflammation, sore throat, eye conditions (Iroquois, Mohawk, Penobscot)
plants.harvest_notes: Widespread; widely cultivated commercially; not at-risk
plants.safety: Topical use very safe; internal use — tannins may cause liver stress with long-term use

plants.habitat: Wetlands, marshes, lake shores — eastern North America and Canada
plants.traditional_uses: Liver/gallbladder, skin conditions, headache, emetic, cathartic (Algonquin, Ojibwe, Cree)
plants.harvest_notes: Wetland species; populations vulnerable to habitat loss; use with extreme caution
plants.safety: TOXIC fresh root — causes severe vomiting; only small prepared doses; avoid in pregnancy, inflammatory bowel conditions

plants.habitat: Moist forest floors, woodland clearings — eastern North America
plants.traditional_uses: Laxative, skin growths (warts, polyps), antitumor, constipation (Iroquois, Cherokee, Penobscot)
plants.harvest_notes: Contains podophyllotoxin (pharmaceutical source); do not harvest root casually
plants.safety: HIGHLY TOXIC except ripe fruit — podophyllin; causes severe GI, neurological damage; topical pharmaceutical use only by professionals; AVOID in pregnancy (abortifacient)

plants.habitat: Dry prairies, rocky outcrops, open woods — central and eastern North America
plants.traditional_uses: Respiratory expectorant, snakebite, fever, stimulant (Seneca, Ojibwe, Cree)
plants.harvest_notes: Populations declining in some areas; commercially harvested for respiratory products
plants.safety: Large doses cause nausea and vomiting; avoid in pregnancy; saponin-rich root

plants.habitat: Moist, shaded woodlands — eastern North America
plants.traditional_uses: Musculoskeletal, joint/tendon repair, digestive conditions, female reproductive (Iroquois, Cherokee, Ojibwe)
plants.harvest_notes: Slow-growing woodland rhizome; prefer cultivated; harvesting root kills plant
plants.safety: Berries TOXIC; root generally safe; avoid in pregnancy

plants.habitat: Woodland edges, river valleys, scrub — widespread North America
plants.traditional_uses: Cough, cold, fever, diarrhea, respiratory, food (pemmican) (Blackfoot, Cree, Ojibwe, widespread)
plants.harvest_notes: Widespread and abundant; not at-risk; widely used as food and medicine
plants.safety: Bark, seeds, leaves contain cyanogenic glycosides — cook berries; don't consume large quantities of seeds or raw leaves

plants.habitat: Coastal sage scrub, dry slopes — Southern California, Baja California
plants.traditional_uses: Ceremonial purification (smudging), respiratory, antiseptic, fever, skin conditions (Chumash, Cahuilla, Tongva)
plants.harvest_notes: Critically overharvested for commercial smudge bundles; sacred to California nations; habitat threatened — do not wild-harvest
plants.safety: Ceremonial smoke inhalation caution; thujone content — avoid as internal tea in large amounts; sacred plant — harvest with permission and respect

plants.habitat: Forest edges, roadsides — eastern North America
plants.traditional_uses: Blood purifier, diuretic, febrifuge, skin conditions, food flavoring (Cherokee, Iroquois, Ojibwe)
plants.harvest_notes: Abundant and weedy; not at-risk
plants.safety: Safrole in root bark — FDA banned as food additive (animal carcinogen); occasional tea use considered low-risk but avoid regular internal consumption

plants.habitat: Moist forests, lakesides, limestone outcrops — northeastern North America and Canada
plants.traditional_uses: Respiratory, fever, rheumatism, antifungal, ceremonial (Ojibwe, Potawatomi, Iroquois)
plants.harvest_notes: Widely available; not at-risk; clip branchlets sustainably
plants.safety: Thujone content — avoid internal use in pregnancy; do not use essential oil internally

plants.habitat: Moist meadows, woodland edges — eastern and central North America
plants.traditional_uses: Liver/gallbladder, laxative, fever, diuretic, liver cleanse (Ojibwe, Seneca, Menominee)
plants.harvest_notes: Not at-risk; grows in cultivation easily
plants.safety: Fresh root strongly cathartic — use aged root only; can cause hepatitis with long-term use

plants.habitat: Rocky slopes, meadows, open woodlands — widespread North America
plants.traditional_uses: Digestive, respiratory, antiseptic, food plant (used by many Plains and Plateau nations)
plants.harvest_notes: Widespread; not at-risk; important food plant
plants.safety: Generally safe; distinguish from toxic wild garlic mimics (Zigadenus/death camas)

plants.habitat: Moist woodlands and meadows — eastern North America
plants.traditional_uses: Astringent, diarrhea, hemorrhage, throat/mouth sores, wounds (Iroquois, Cherokee, Ojibwe)
plants.harvest_notes: Widespread and not at-risk; grows readily in woodland gardens
plants.safety: High tannin — safe short-term; avoid excessive long-term internal use

plants.habitat: Moist meadows, roadsides, lakeshores — widespread northern North America
plants.traditional_uses: Ceremonial purification, braided for prayer offerings, sacred smudging plant (virtually all nations with distribution overlap)
plants.harvest_notes: Sacred cultural importance; harvest only what is needed with permission and respect; populations declining in some areas due to overharvesting for commercial smudge trade
plants.safety: Generally safe; coumarin content — do not consume large amounts internally

plants.habitat: Sandy soils, open areas — eastern and central USA
plants.traditional_uses: Fever, stomachaches, chest congestion, colds, antifungal (Chippewa, Delaware, Menominee)
plants.harvest_notes: Not at-risk; grows in sandy soils from seed easily
plants.safety: High thymol content — safe at culinary doses; avoid very large amounts; not for pregnancy

plants.habitat: Rocky slopes, open forests, desert edges — widespread North America
plants.traditional_uses: Respiratory, urinary, ceremonial, analgesic, antimicrobial (Navajo, Apache, Ojibwe — one of the most-used medicinal plants across nations)
plants.harvest_notes: Widespread; not at-risk
plants.safety: Avoid with kidney disease; do not use internally during pregnancy (uterine stimulant); essential oil toxic internally

plants.habitat: Moist soils, streamsides — widespread North America
plants.traditional_uses: Fever, pain, ceremonial smoking mix (kinnikinnick), food (berry), diarrhea (Ojibwe, Blackfoot, Cree, widespread)
plants.harvest_notes: Abundant and widespread; not at-risk
plants.safety: Generally safe; bark tannins — moderate internal use

plants.habitat: Wet woods, marsh edges, moist habitats — eastern North America
plants.traditional_uses: Digestive, respiratory, fever, rheumatism, colds (Iroquois, Ojibwe, Algonquin)
plants.harvest_notes: Local populations can be vulnerable; do not confuse with poison hemlock (Conium) or water hemlock (Cicuta)
plants.safety: CRITICAL IDENTIFICATION: can be confused with deadly Conium maculatum and Cicuta spp. — expert ID required; furanocoumarins cause phototoxicity; avoid in pregnancy

plants.habitat: Boreal forests, moist uplands — northeastern North America and Canada
plants.traditional_uses: Respiratory, wounds (resin as antiseptic/sealant), rheumatism, cold, ceremonial (Ojibwe, Cree, Algonquin)
plants.harvest_notes: Abundant boreal forest tree; not at-risk
plants.safety: Generally safe; resin skin sensitization possible with prolonged use

plants.habitat: Dry open woods, fields — eastern North America
plants.traditional_uses: Insect repellent, headaches, digestive, colds, emmenagogue (Iroquois, Cherokee, Chippewa)
plants.harvest_notes: Widespread; not at-risk
plants.safety: AVOID IN PREGNANCY — powerful emmenagogue and abortifacient; pulegone toxic in large doses; safe as occasional tea; do NOT use essential oil internally

plants.scientific_name: Arnica montana L. / A. cordifolia Hook.
plants.habitat: Mountain meadows — Rocky Mountains, Pacific Northwest, western Canada
plants.traditional_uses: Topical: bruises, muscle pain, sprains, arthritis (Okanagan, Carrier, Stoney)
plants.harvest_notes: A. montana heavily commercialized; native species at-risk in some mountain areas
plants.safety: External use only — toxic internally; causes severe gastroenteritis; avoid on broken skin; allergic reactions in some Asteraceae-sensitive individuals

plants.habitat: Riverbanks, moist forests — boreal Canada, Alaska, northern USA
plants.traditional_uses: Respiratory, skin conditions, wounds, antiseptic, rheumatism (Cree, Ojibwe, Dene)
plants.harvest_notes: Widespread boreal species; not at-risk; gather fallen buds when possible
plants.safety: Contains salicylates — avoid with aspirin sensitivity; generally safe topically

plants.habitat: Wet meadows, marshes, roadsides — eastern and central North America
plants.traditional_uses: Nervine, fever, cough, liver, stomach upset, diuretic (Iroquois, Ojibwe, Menominee)
plants.harvest_notes: Widespread wetland plant; not at-risk
plants.safety: Generally safe; mild sedative; avoid in pregnancy

plants.habitat: Dry prairies, roadsides, disturbed areas — central and western North America
plants.traditional_uses: Respiratory (asthma, bronchitis), poison ivy rash, skin conditions (Blackfoot, Pawnee, Cheyenne)
plants.harvest_notes: Abundant in prairie and disturbed areas; not at-risk
plants.safety: Resinous — large internal doses toxic; standard herbal doses safe; avoid in kidney disease

plants.habitat: Moist mountain meadows, streambanks — eastern and western North America
plants.traditional_uses: Ceremonial, paralysis, rheumatism, lice treatment (historical use by multiple nations) — mostly avoided now
plants.harvest_notes: Do not harvest or use without expert guidance
plants.safety: HIGHLY TOXIC — steroidal alkaloids; small doses cause cardiac and respiratory depression; modern medicinal use nearly abandoned; historical interest only; do not wild-harvest

plants.habitat: Dry grasslands, coastal bluffs, hillsides — California and Pacific coast
plants.traditional_uses: Mild sedative, pain relief, sleep, headache (Costanoan, Pomo, Luiseño)
plants.harvest_notes: California state flower — illegal to uproot in California; leaves/flowers may be harvested; not a poppy family opioid
plants.safety: No opioid alkaloids — completely different from opium poppy; generally safe; mild sedative; avoid in pregnancy

plants.habitat: Dry rocky slopes, sagebrush steppe — western North America
plants.traditional_uses: Antiviral, respiratory illness, flu, pneumonia (Paiute, Shoshone, Thompson)
plants.harvest_notes: Very slow growing root (decades); harvest only from abundant stands; prefer cultivated
plants.safety: Furanocoumarins — severe photosensitivity; rare skin rash reaction in some users; avoid in pregnancy

plants.habitat: Sandy dry soils, rocky outcrops — eastern North America
plants.traditional_uses: Diarrhea, poison ivy rash, astringent, fever (Algonquin, Mohegan, Ojibwe)
plants.harvest_notes: Not at-risk; not a true fern despite common name
plants.safety: Generally safe; avoid in pregnancy

plants.habitat: Woodland edges, thickets — eastern and central North America
plants.traditional_uses: Toothache, poor circulation, rheumatism, digestive, respiratory (Ojibwe, Potawatomi, Cherokee)
plants.harvest_notes: Widespread shrub; not at-risk
plants.safety: May interact with blood thinners (stimulates circulation); avoid in pregnancy; numbing quality from chewing bark

plants.habitat: Burned areas, clearings, roadsides — all of North America
plants.traditional_uses: Anti-inflammatory, gut healing, skin wounds, food plant (Cree, Dene, Ojibwe, widely used)
plants.harvest_notes: Extremely abundant post-disturbance species; not at-risk; edible and medicinal
plants.safety: Very safe; young shoots edible; tea generally safe

plants.habitat: Prairies, streambanks, disturbed soils — central and western USA
plants.traditional_uses: Sore throat, cough, digestive soother, fever, toothache (Lakota, Cheyenne, Comanche)
plants.harvest_notes: Not at-risk; widespread prairie species
plants.safety: Long-term large doses raise blood pressure (glycyrrhizin); avoid with hypertension, heart failure, edema, pregnancy

plants.habitat: Wet places, streambanks — widespread North America and Canada
plants.traditional_uses: Digestive, headache, colds, nausea, ceremonial (Ojibwe, Cree, Blackfoot)
plants.harvest_notes: Widespread and abundant; not at-risk
plants.safety: Generally safe; avoid large amounts in infants and young children; galactagogue in moderate doses

plants.habitat: Dry to moist open areas — widespread North America
plants.traditional_uses: Heart tonic, diuretic, fever, fiber plant (Cherokee, Iroquois, Ojibwe)
plants.harvest_notes: Contains cardiac glycosides — only for experienced practitioners
plants.safety: TOXIC — cardiac glycosides; do not self-administer; traditional use with strict guidance only; milky sap irritating

plants.habitat: Open slopes, roadsides, disturbed areas — widespread North America
plants.traditional_uses: Coughs, lung complaints, rheumatism, ceremonial smoking (Chippewa, Blackfoot, Thompson)
plants.harvest_notes: Widespread and abundant; not at-risk
plants.safety: Generally safe

plants.habitat: Lawns, meadows, disturbed ground — widespread North America
plants.traditional_uses: Wound healing, sore throats, anti-inflammatory, diarrhea (Ojibwe, Iroquois, Cherokee)
plants.harvest_notes: Extremely abundant lawn weed; not at-risk
plants.safety: Very safe; edible herb

plants.habitat: Fields, roadsides, disturbed soils — eastern and central North America
plants.traditional_uses: Skin conditions, inflammation, obesity treatment, food plant, bruise poultice (Cherokee, Ojibwe)
plants.harvest_notes: Abundant weedy biennial; not at-risk
plants.safety: Generally safe; seed oil may lower seizure threshold in epileptics; avoid with phenothiazines

plants.habitat: Moist slopes, streambanks — eastern North America
plants.traditional_uses: Stomachache, fevers, antiseptic, diuretic (Iroquois/Oswego peoples)
plants.harvest_notes: Not at-risk; popular garden plant
plants.safety: Safe as culinary tea; avoid excess in pregnancy

plants.habitat: Sandy soils, desert grasslands — Southwest USA and Great Plains
plants.traditional_uses: Stomach disorders, colds, respiratory (Pueblo, Navajo, Zuni)
plants.harvest_notes: Abundant desert species; not at-risk
plants.safety: Contains aromatic camphor oils; avoid in pregnancy; moderate doses only

plants.scientific_name: Ericameria nauseosa (Pall. ex Pursh) G.L. Nesom
plants.habitat: Arid plains, desert shrub — western North America
plants.traditional_uses: Stomach disorders, coughs, headache, chewing gum (Navajo, Pueblo, Paiute)
plants.harvest_notes: Extremely abundant in arid West; not at-risk
plants.safety: Generally safe as tea

plants.scientific_name: Fallugia paradoxa (D. Don) Endl. ex Torr.
plants.habitat: Desert slopes, arroyos — Southwest USA and northwest Mexico
plants.traditional_uses: Cough, hair care, rheumatic joints, ceremonial preparations (Navajo, Puebloans, Apache)
plants.harvest_notes: Abundant in Southwest desert scrub; not at-risk
plants.safety: Generally safe

plants.habitat: Desert and semi-arid scrub — Southwest USA
plants.traditional_uses: Urinary disorders, diarrhea, venereal disease, kidney tonic, skin conditions (Zuni, Navajo, Pueblo)
plants.harvest_notes: Abundant in Southwest desert; not at-risk; North American species have very low ephedrine
plants.safety: North American Ephedra species have much lower ephedrine than Asian species (E. sinica); generally safe as traditional tea; do not confuse with Chinese ma huang supplements

plants.habitat: Dry plains, desert grasslands — western North America
plants.traditional_uses: Postpartum care, snakebite, fever, headache, menstrual (Navajo, Zuni, Pueblo)
plants.harvest_notes: Extremely abundant dry-land species; not at-risk
plants.safety: Toxic in large quantities to livestock; moderate human doses safe as tea

plants.habitat: Desert flats, roadsides — Southwest USA and Mexico
plants.traditional_uses: Mucilaginous soothing, skin, digestive, eye conditions (Navajo, Hopi, Zuni)
plants.harvest_notes: Abundant in dry Southwest; not at-risk
plants.safety: Leaf hairs can irritate eyes and mucous membranes; prepare as tea carefully; generally safe

plants.habitat: Dry prairies, mesa tops — southwestern USA and southern Great Plains
plants.traditional_uses: Daily beverage-medicine; digestive, kidney tonic, general wellness (Navajo, Hopi, Zuni, Apache)
plants.harvest_notes: Not at-risk; sustainably harvested commercially by SW communities
plants.safety: Very safe as daily beverage tea; antioxidant properties

plants.habitat: Dry, rocky slopes, sage steppe — Rocky Mountains and Pacific Northwest
plants.traditional_uses: Respiratory, wounds, food plant, fuel for smoke healing (Nez Perce, Cheyenne, Salish, Kootenai)
plants.harvest_notes: Very long-lived root — can live 50+ years; harvest only from abundant patches; leaves preferred over root
plants.safety: Generally safe; resinous root in large amounts may cause digestive upset

plants.habitat: Moist soils, streamsides, coast ranges — Pacific Northwest and California
plants.traditional_uses: Lymphatic disorders, tuberculosis, skin irritations, antitumor, poison oak (Blackfoot, Quinault, Salish)
plants.harvest_notes: Common riparian tree; not at-risk; take bark from fallen or pruned branches
plants.safety: Generally safe; bark contains salicin and betulin; large doses may cause GI upset

plants.habitat: Woodlands, roadsides — eastern North America
plants.traditional_uses: Diarrhea, fever, urinary, hemostatic (Iroquois, Cherokee, Ojibwe, Potawatomi)
plants.harvest_notes: Widespread; not at-risk
plants.safety: Generally safe; tannin-rich; avoid prolonged internal use

plants.habitat: Rich moist hardwood forests — eastern North America
plants.traditional_uses: Digestive, cold, spring tonic, antiseptic, food plant (Iroquois, Cherokee, Ojibwe)
plants.harvest_notes: Slow-growing; populations under pressure from over-harvesting; harvest only 1 bulb in 10; prefer leaf harvest only
plants.safety: Generally safe; strong sulfur compounds; do not confuse with toxic lily-of-the-valley (Convallaria) or false hellebore

plants.habitat: Open woodlands, riverbanks — eastern USA
plants.traditional_uses: Purgative, toothache, nausea, eye wash (Cherokee, Osage)
plants.harvest_notes: Slow growing; prefer cultivated; populations declining in some states
plants.safety: Toxic in large doses; emetic and cathartic; avoid in pregnancy

plants.habitat: Wet alkaline soils, stream margins — California to Texas
plants.traditional_uses: Antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, respiratory, skin, wounds, ceremonial (Cahuilla, Diegueno, Yaqui)
plants.harvest_notes: Riparian wetland species; habitat threatened; prefer cultivated
plants.safety: Generally safe; large doses may be irritating

plants.habitat: Rocky slopes, desert scrub — western North America
plants.traditional_uses: Sore throat, skin astringent, food (tart berries), cold sores, diarrhea (Pueblo, Navajo, Paiute)
plants.harvest_notes: Abundant in dry western habitats; not at-risk
plants.safety: Edible berries (sour, not toxic); not related to poison sumac (Toxicodendron) — safe

plants.habitat: Sandy desert soils — Southwest USA
plants.traditional_uses: Sore throat, burns, rashes, antibacterial, tanning agent (Navajo, Zuni, Pueblo)
plants.harvest_notes: Abundant in sandy Southwest soils; not at-risk; commercially harvested for tannin
plants.safety: High tannin and oxalate content — excessive internal use can cause kidney stones; topical use safe

plants.habitat: Desert flats, dry grasslands — western North America
plants.traditional_uses: Digestive, food plant, ceremonial ash for corn bread (Navajo, Hopi, Zuni)
plants.harvest_notes: Very abundant desert shrub; not at-risk
plants.safety: Generally safe; high salt content — moderate use

plants.habitat: Moist woodland habitats — eastern North America
plants.traditional_uses: Fever, syphilis, cough, toothache (Iroquois, Cherokee — limited documentation)
plants.harvest_notes: Woodland species; harvest cautiously; limited commercial use
plants.safety: Limited data; use with caution

plants.habitat: Boreal forests, bogs, wet areas — Canada and northern USA
plants.traditional_uses: Respiratory, scurvy prevention (vitamin C), ceremonial, wounds (Cree, Ojibwe, Innu)
plants.harvest_notes: Vast boreal distribution; not at-risk; clip only small amounts from any single tree
plants.safety: Very safe as tea; high vitamin C in fresh needles

plants.habitat: Coniferous forests — northern North America
plants.traditional_uses: Mild epilepsy treatment (Nlaka'pamux of British Columbia — rare, specific use)
plants.harvest_notes: DO NOT harvest — extremely vulnerable mycorrhizal orchid; dies if root or soil disturbed; protected in many jurisdictions; document only
plants.safety: No current medicinal use recommended — conservation concern overrides any use

plants.habitat: Dry prairies, open disturbed areas — central and western North America
plants.traditional_uses: Ceremonial smudging, fever, menstrual, antiparasitic, rheumatism (Blackfoot, Lakota, Crow — very widespread)
plants.harvest_notes: Abundant on plains; not at-risk; one of the most important ceremonial plants of the Great Plains
plants.safety: Thujone content — avoid in pregnancy; avoid large internal doses; safe for smudging and light tea

plants.habitat: Alpine meadows, moist mountain habitats — western North America
plants.traditional_uses: Digestive, diarrhea, food plant (cooked roots eaten by many western nations) (Blackfoot, Nez Perce)
plants.harvest_notes: Alpine meadow species; harvest sustainably; leave most plants
plants.safety: Edible; high in tannins — moderate use; safe

plants.habitat: Dry prairies and rocky soils — eastern and central USA
plants.traditional_uses: Fever, colds, malaria-like fevers, burns (Cherokee, Catawba, Winnebago)
plants.harvest_notes: Not at-risk; prairie restoration plant
plants.safety: Generally safe; distinct from European feverfew (Tanacetum parthenium); no migraine evidence for this species

plants.habitat: Streambanks, wet meadows — eastern North America
plants.traditional_uses: Liver/gallbladder, digestive bitters, worms, fevers (Cherokee, Iroquois)
plants.harvest_notes: Wetland species; not at-risk in most areas
plants.safety: Bitter; large doses cathartic; avoid in pregnancy

plants.scientific_name: Salix spp. (S. alba, S. nigra, S. lucida)
plants.habitat: Wetlands, streambanks, moist lowlands — widespread North America
plants.traditional_uses: Pain relief (salicin = aspirin precursor), fever, anti-inflammatory, headache (virtually all Nations in range)
plants.harvest_notes: Abundant; not at-risk as a group; clip from branches — do not ring-bark
plants.safety: Avoid with aspirin sensitivity; blood-thinning effect; do not combine with anticoagulants; avoid in children (Reye's syndrome risk, as with aspirin)

plants.habitat: Fields, meadows, roadsides — widespread (introduced from Europe, naturalized throughout)
plants.traditional_uses: Respiratory, hot flashes, skin conditions, blood purifier (Cherokee and others — adopted into Indigenous use post-contact)
plants.harvest_notes: Widespread introduced species; not at-risk; widely cultivated
plants.safety: Contains isoflavones; avoid with hormone-sensitive conditions; not for pregnancy in medicinal amounts

plants.habitat: Disturbed areas, roadsides — widespread (introduced from Eurasia)
plants.traditional_uses: Blood purifier, liver tonic, skin disorders, diuretic (Iroquois, Ojibwe — adopted)
plants.harvest_notes: Abundant and widespread introduced weed; not at-risk; root edible (gobo)
plants.safety: Generally safe; avoid in pregnancy; may interact with blood thinners and diabetes medications

plants.scientific_name: Valeriana officinalis L. / V. sitchensis Bong.
plants.habitat: Moist meadows, streambanks — northern USA and Canada (native spp.)
plants.traditional_uses: Sedative, anxiety, insomnia, nervous tension (Menominee, Pacific NW nations)
plants.harvest_notes: Not at-risk; widely cultivated
plants.safety: Sedative — avoid with other sedatives, alcohol; may cause vivid dreams; avoid in pregnancy; morning drowsiness at high doses

plants.habitat: Fields, roadsides — widespread eastern and central USA
plants.traditional_uses: Scurvy prevention, diuretic, cough, digestive, food plant (Rappahannock, Cherokee)
plants.harvest_notes: Very common weed; not at-risk; edible
plants.safety: Safe as food; pungent flavor; large doses in pregnancy — avoid as medicinal

plants.habitat: Desert washes, disturbed soils — Southwest USA
plants.traditional_uses: Ceremonial visions, anesthesia for bone-setting, pain relief under strict ritual control (Pueblo, Zuni, Chumash, Navajo)
plants.harvest_notes: DO NOT harvest or use without traditional ceremonial guidance — this is sacred and extremely dangerous
plants.safety: HIGHLY TOXIC — tropane alkaloids (atropine, scopolamine); fatal in small doses above therapeutic threshold; NO self-administration; deaths documented

plants.habitat: Hardwood forests — eastern North America
plants.traditional_uses: Snakebite, rheumatism, nervous conditions, malaria (Algonquin, Iroquois — distinct from menopausal use in NA004)
plants.harvest_notes: Same species as NA004 (Actaea racemosa); cross-reference; UpS At-Risk List
plants.safety: See NA004; avoid in pregnancy; potential hepatotoxicity
